channel.title: Game Development on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Game Development on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Game Development As An Identity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development as an identity And

this is probably going to be a fun Friday video because it’s going to be about game development, but it’s going to be kind of personal, Not 100% sure where it’s going to go, maybe a little rambly, Sorry about that. So 5 years ago — right now it’s January of 2026.. Sorry, there’s a fire truck outside. That’s interesting, so it’s January of 2026, and back in May of 2020, so over five years ago, I semi-retired And what that means is I went from being an employee at Obsidian to being a contractor And I consulted at other companies too — all part-time. It wasn’t what I was doing full-time. This let me see a lot of different game development processes. It was fascinating to do it I actually got to work on a lot of toys. I have two videos on semi-retirement, One talking about semi-retirement and the other one talking about why I even did it The whole time.

item.title: Brainstorming Example
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a brainstorming example.

And by that I mean two years ago I did a video on how I like to brainstorm. I used to run brainstorming meetings at almost every company I worked at, where I’d gather people together, cross-disciplinary people who wanted to, and it was all volunteer. If you didn’t want to go to the brainstorming session, you certainly didn’t have to, and we were going to talk about either an idea I was going to present or I was there to collect ideas. You know, pay people, give me examples of what kind of games you want to make. I mean, that’s how Fallout got started.

item.title: Game Development As A Family Car Trip
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game development.

As a family car trip, Bear with me on this. I’ve done a lot of talking about game development over the last couple years. I’ve talked about teams and all, how to organize them and how to look for good team members. I’ve talked about game directors and how to be a good one and pitfalls of being a game director. I’ve talked about design pillars. I’ve talked about, I’ve even dived in like intent, like this is people’s intent in making a game as opposed to people’s interpretation. I’ve even tried to explain how design choices are, even made at the most fundamental level.

item.title: Zen And Game Development
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Zen and game development.

I almost called this Zen and the art of game development after that book I like. But game development is not an art. I’ve talked about that. It’s art and science smooshed together. No side is trumps the other, so I just want to call it game development. Also, I’m gonna talk about Zen, but I want to point out that I’m not religious, Wasn’t raised religiously. I call myself secular, but I did take a lot of religion classes. My last year in undergraduate school I mentioned I was an engineer and I had six general education electives which I had used for things like astrophysics — you know what an engineer thinks as a hobby. But I was so interested in religion that I used several of them. I took a Greek mythology class. By the way, religious people get kind of upset when you think of mythology as religion, but deal with it I took a, comparative religion class. And then I really, really liked Buddhism, really liked Buddhism, and I took a class just on Buddhism. I have some of my books from that but most of them I’ve lost or given away over the years And then related to that. One of my favorite channels to watch is a philosophy channel.

item.title: Staying Healthy While Developing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about recent findings in fossilized endothermic theorapsids.

Now, these studies are really exciting because — oh, wait a minute, Nope, that’ll have to wait. I want to answer a question from a master member about staying healthy while developing games. This came from master member RoboCg, who literally asked this in my previous video about being happy, and he asked: “Do you have ideas about being healthy? From what I can see, a lot of my fellow game devs — mostly artists — are not having a healthy work life balance, which has caused some serious health rellated issues. Maybe you have some stories — Ergonomics might be a side theme for that. It feels like most people neglect it.

item.title: "Impossible" Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about impossible ideas.

I am going to talk about what I mean by impossible at some point in the video and what it probably really means, but I think it’d be more fun for me to talk about examples of that before I say let’s dig into impossible. But this is a question from Fear the clown who, in my video for Unique roles for players video, which I’ll link below, asked: the idea of a boss having its own save game is wild. Can you go into more detail about your thought process for that boss fight?

item.title: Hypothetical Game Jam
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a hypothetical game, Jam.

What’s happened? A master member on the channel asked me a question, so I had to stop what I was doing to answer it right in the middle of the afternoon. I was, I was doing some work and I said no, I got to stop.

Super duper asked: can you imagine you’re in a game Jam? You’ve got 48 Hours. What game are you making? What’s the story setting and mechanics? How do you keep your workload light while making a robust game? And just to complete the image, the theme of the game Jam is your hobbies, so it has to be a game about something you love, like chocolate or baking, or game design or whatever.

item.title: How Much Devs Should Play
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how much devs should play.

This is kind of a continuation of a video I made a month ago — month ago, almost a year ago — called playing games, and I’ll link it below. But it’s me answering a question from viewer Amir husin magudi 388, who asked: what is your opinion on keeping a clean pallet?

As in your working on something, don’t look at what other people are doing. If you do look at what other people are doing, it might narrow your creativity. If you do look at what they’re doing, you may learn things you never come up with. I guess this also goes for playing a game blind. Guess what.

item.title: Great External Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about great external ideas, and by that I mean ideas that came from outside the development team, that were really good and worked and ended up in the game.

This actually was a question from brains and beats, who asked: has there been a time when some person from high up or just not on the dev or design team brought in an idea that was actually great, and I love this question because, while I frequently told stories that have people off the team not being shown in the best light, I love that this is a way to Showcase some really good ideas that came from people who weren’t on the development team. I’m going to define the development team as people who worked on the game as their daily job. You know every — everyone from the game director down to the most Junior person, is on the development team.

item.title: Playing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about playing games now, probably one of the most frequent questions or question types I get on this channel.

If people want to know what games I’ve played or have I played a particular game, or will I play a particular game and live stream it, or have I played a particular mod, or have I played a particular tabletop game and what I think of it. Um, and I understand why people are asking me that you’ve played it and you either really like it or really hate it for some of you and you want to talk about it, and I get that. But the primary goal of this channel, as I’ve said multiple times, is I want to talk about what game development is like and what my experience with game development is like, in case you’re thinking about going into game development or you’re in game development, because there are just a lot of issues that arise and you find yourself second guessing yourself. You. Think you’re alone in all this and I want to point out you’re not.

item.title: Game Development Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development roles.

Now I could have sworn I had talked about this before, but the closest I got was programming — programming roles. But I have talked a lot about what makes a good game director, traits I look for in producers and challenges that face, or challenges that programmers, artists or designers have to face. So there’s a lot of related videos and I’ve linked them all in the description, but I’m going to do this one now. I’m answering Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you have a video explaining the job in game development process and what they do: artists, programmers, producers, leads, designers and all that. I was having questions about that. I know that every studio is different. Could you explain it to me? I know it might be silly, their names are self-explanatory but would mean a lot.

item.title: A Non-Game Design Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a noname design spec.

It’s Friday, Funday, so this has nothing to do with game development, but it does have to do with a specification, but it’s not for a game. I’ve talked about how, when I play games, I sometimes can’t turn off the design part of my brain. So I’ll be playing a game and i’ be like H, this UI isn’t very good, or uh, you know, I can’t believe they put this sound effect and it doesn’t match. Or sometimes, even though I’m not very much of an art person, I will occasionally see a piece of art, whether it’s a piece of scenery or a creature, and go really with all the other things they put in. They put in this thing which doesn’t seem to match.

item.title: Game Development Tunnel Vision
item.description: Sal SE, Tim, par gotcha. I bet you right now some of you are reaching for that YouTube translate button and some of you were thinking, whoa, Tim’s French accent is terrible, trust me, that’s been said. No, I’m doing this video about game development, tunnel vision, because it’s one of the things that occurred to me. I get a lot of questions about a lot of things and early on, when there were less of you, I would answer and say, oh, that’s not something I know or I can’t talk about it I did do a video on things I can’t or won’t answer, but since then there’s been a lot more questions that Encompass a lot more things, even about game development or tangentially related game development, that I literally cannot answer. I either don’t know it or I’m not qualified to answer.

item.title: The Business Side Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the business side of game development.

This is an answer to a question by Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you could explain the business side of the game industry. I know you started troa. I would love to hear the business stuff about it I know you have a video about troa, but could you go further into the money aspect of it? Could you explain starting up a studio and dealing with hiring people, choosing and approaching Publishers, getting funding for the game, Insurance, banking, PR, office space costs, advertising and other things people wouldn’t think of?

item.title: Idea Battles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about idea battles.

This comes from a question asked by triplicata, who asked: hey, Tim, bit of a long-winded question I’ve had for a while now. In previous videos you’ve talked about sitting in a room for a long time having arguments in quotes about creative decisions. However, they’re not really fights where people are upset, but rather passionate debates where people go to bat for their ideas. I love those types of discussions. I like to call them idea battles. My question is: do you have any favorite idea battles or ones that stand out to you? Times where you enjoyed the conversation, times where you thought you were right but it turned out the other idea was better, or maybe times where the discussion led to a new idea that was better than the ones originally argued?

item.title: Research
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about research, specifically the research that you do when you first start a game.

I get a very detailed question on the channel from Lazar satvic, who asked: this might be a very lengthy topic, but I’m interested to hear you talk about the research phase of game development. I have a couple of sub questions about it.

Obviously, research is very important for games that are set into historical settings or games that want to accurately depict real modern-day cities. So how important was research in Fallout, Arcanum, in The Outer Worlds, which aren’t tied down that way? Does research only happen during the early stages of development or does it last throughout production? Can there be additional research during the later stages of production if the need arises? Which are some of the things you personally researched while designing your games?

item.title: The "Best" OS For Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the best operating system to make games.

For now. This is an answer to the question that Romo bomb asked, which is: hey, Tim, can you please talk about the best OS to use, in your experience, for programming and designing? Does it even matter for the user in those professional grounds? Yes, but I’m not going to talk about what I think the best one is to be doing that work in, because that’s subjective. Some people like Mac OS, some people like Windows, some people like Linux and there’s a million osses out there. I’m going to answer in terms of pure practicality.

item.title: Scenes From A Life Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you scenes from a life spent in game development.

Let me tell you where this came from. I’m thinking, so do not hold me to this — I’m thinking about writing a book on game development. Basically, get rid of all the Memoir stuff, that gets people riled up and you know why don’t you give me credit, why did you tell the story about me? BL, just stuff about here’s how I recommend making games.

item.title: Game Design Specification Sections
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about all the different design specifications that a single game may need, because about a month ago I gave an example of how to write a design specification and gypsum generation asks: what are all the design specifications that a game might need?

Okay, that is a very long answer, so grab your coffee. Also, I’m going to put a big caveat on this and say the answer really depends on the game you’re making, because different games will have different sections in their design specification. So let’s pretend we’re making a fantasy RPG and I will tell you what the design specs should include. So I’ll break it down into. I’ll go down to what all the main sections of the design specification are and then for each one of those sections I’ll talk about subsections they should have. Why don’t you take a look at my writing design specifications for exactly the kind of details you will have to put in each one of those subsections?

item.title: Writing Design Specifications
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to write design specifications.

Specifically, I’m going to answer a question from Thor RoR TV who asks: I was trying to write a design document for a solo project I’m working on and was wondering if you had any examples of how to properly write a specification that might be given to a programmer or an artist. Also, are there any pitfalls to avoid when writing speci ifications? That is a very good question, thoro TV, and it’s also a little hard to answer because there’s a wild, wildly huge range of design specifications. You might be writing a might be writing, you might be writing a system mechanic. You might be writing a user interface, you might be writing a — um, some narrative guidelines.

item.title: Stop Making Excuses
item.description:
It’s me, Tim, and it’s tough love today on the Canon games Channel because I want all of you to stop making excuses and start making games.

Consider this my shilou, just do it. Motivational speech video, but I’ve told you, guys, I read the comments and I’m reading comments with so many different reasons that people are rationalizing why they’re not working on a game. So let’s walk through some of those and then at the end I’ll give you some ideas for games you can make. That will help you get started because they’re compartmentalized nicely anyway.

item.title: Fast Fail
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about a development methodology called Fast fail.

Ironically, I used to do fast fail 30 years ago, before I knew what it was called that, and I’m doing it now with my own little fun in-house games. But it hasn’t been something done recently and I want to talk about it because I really think fast fail is a good way of making games and maybe if you’re thinking about making your prototype, this may help you put it together. So what’s called Fast fail these days is something that I usually just call rapid prototyping, and if you watch the video I have on stages that um game development goes through, prototyping is one of the early stages where you quickly put together a level just to see if a mechanic works or just to see if the level size is good or the sight lines are good or whatever you’re trying to do. You do it before you have final art and final code to do rapid prototyping, you have to be in the mindset that you view failure as a good thing, that you’re not upset when something fails. You’re happy that, with as little time and resource investment as possible, you discovered something you were about to do isn’t going to work.

item.title: Game Development Caution, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to do part two of game development caution, because that video got a lot of views and a lot of comments and I kind of want to talk about it.

Yeah, I know the lighting’s weird. It is just dark in Seattle all the time. Sometimes today’s a dark day, so I just turned on the overhead light.

See how this goes, so I did video on game developer caution — game development caution — and on the one hand, there was a lot of really good discussion about it and that was great. I loved that there was discussion, because that’s kind of what the game development business needs. It needs people talking about things, talking about processes, talking about problems, just telling people. This is what it’s like, so you can make an informed decision if this is the job you want to do.

item.title: Let's Talk About Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk to you about ideas.

I’ve mentioned that I have notebooks full of ideas. There’s one right here on my desk right now. There’s a whole like half a dozen of them up on this bookshelf. They are filled to the brim with ideas. Some of them are ideas that I fleshed out over several Pages.

Some are just literally a sentence. Hey, wouldn’t it be cool if a Game feature did XYZ? I still have people in comments and direct messages offering me their ideas. In some case they’ve even put together a little demo, or they have a script or something and they want me to review them, and I’m always turning it down, and I will tell you why it’s a dangerous thing to do.

item.title: My University Game Development Class
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about my university class in game design.

This isn’t a new thing. This isn’t something I’m teaching. I want to tell you about a class I taught at the University of California, Irvine, in the spring of 99. Now I’ve got my glasses on because I actually found my course notes. I have an overview, which is what I’m going to go over, but I actually have the individual lecture notes for each one of these.

item.title: School As Preparation For Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about school as preparation for game development.

Now I’ve touched on this in many videos. I liked being in school.

I know some of you don’t. I know some of you think college is a waste of time and for some of you it would be. For some of you it will not be.

So let me just talk about how School and when I started, even in high school, was sort of a preparation for my whole game Development Career. So let’s start in high school. As I mentioned, is where I first learned about DND and I started playing regular campaigns with my D and D friends. True, I did hear about DN D and play for the first time with naval officers from my mom’s office, but my regular DND experiences started in high school and it wasn’t restricted to just the weekends when we would play. We would spend the whole week talking about what we had done, what we wanted to do house. Rules, things we’d Chang in the game. It was really exciting because there was nothing like it before. DN D was a brand new thing.

item.title: Game Development Caution
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about something that, for a lack of a better phrase, I’m going to call game development caution.

Before I start and tell you three different stories — they’re a little different, but then I’ll tell you what I’m thinking about. When we made Fallout, towards the end, when we were really trying to get it out, we had two white boards. One white board had a list of features or content that weren’t done yet, that really needed to get in, and the other whiteboard had a list of what the 10 most egregious bugs were, and next to each person, next to each one of these on these two different whiteboards, were listed the person who was assigned to it. We did it in a whiteboard like this so that people could come in the morning and look at it because this predated jira or Confluence or anything like that. They could come in, look at the Whiteboard and go: oh, I see something I need to jump on worked.

item.title: My Bad Game Development Skills
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game development tasks that I’m bad at.

So I thought I’d talk about those game development skills that have eluded me over many decades and that I finally resolved myself to. You’re not going to learn these, you’re not going to get better at these. It’s better to let someone else do it.

So let me talk about that, so, first of all, I think the biggest thing when I look back at my games, it’s adding or editing, pairing away features. If there’s one problem I always have is I want too many system mechanics in one game. I just when I start thinking it’s like I want this, one makes sense, this, I want this, one makes sense, but only if you have this one to balance it. We need more of this. There’s hey, I’m worried about this kind of character being made and not being supported, so we need to add this, and this in every one of my games. It adds up to too many system mechanics. I think Arcanum was probably the ultimate expression of that, where if there was an idea that anybody had, it went into Arcanum and Arcanum just had everything. I mean it had.

item.title: Features: From Ideas To Implementation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from Wyatt’s Vlog who asks: could you talk about the steps you take when exploring and implementing a feature?

Good question, also one that I held off a while on answering because it touches a lot of other videos: the three Challenge videos: challenging facing designers, challenges facing produce programmers and challenges facing artists. I’ll touch upon those, also, the problem of doing credits: I’ll touch upon that as well. So let me tell you how I go about exploring and implementing a feature in a game. So I’m gonna assume the game is already roughly designed, possibly already underway, and you have an idea for feature for it, because this is very different. This is a very different process than coming up with an idea for a game altogether.

item.title: Game Development Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game development time.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions from a lot of people. This is maybe the most commonly asked question, at least in the last few weeks, because I’ve gotten at least I don’t know four or five of these. Uh geomancer HT asks: please talk about the.

It’s ready when it’s done and a delayed game is eventually good. Tetramir 1 asks: uh, talking about a length of development would be interesting subject, because teams have gotten bigger but games take longer too. Yeti catcher 1780 asked: do you think games coming out under shorter deadlines means only the best things get put in the game, so it’s better, or do you think it’s always better for them to have huge development Cycles and our own bite your own teeth asked? Here’s another one: when to cut, when to polish, what to look out for, when a feature won’t make the cut, how do you get it past the finish line? These are pretty much.

item.title: Game Development: Then vs. Now
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to ask a question from, or answer a question — I keep doing that from — Jeremy Mason 9802 asks: do you think game development is harder or Worse nowadays compared to, say, the 90s, or is it better or the same?

Here’s how I’m going to answer that, because I think that is a very nuanced question that needs a nuanced answer. So here’s what I’m going to do: I’m going to tell you some things that I think are better, I’m going to tell you some things that I think are worse, and I’m going to tell you some things that go either way, mainly because, depending on you, may think it’s a better thing, you may think it’s a worse thing or you may not even care about that thing. So let me tell you, let me start with what I think is better, and also, I think it’s funny that you said the 90s, since I started in 1981, the Whole Decade there. Uh, the 80s, where it was.

item.title: Brainstorming
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about brainstorming, basically my thoughts on it, how we did it, what worked, what didn’t.

So, basically, when I talk about brainstorming, I’m usually talking about a meeting where we get together and I explicitly tell people it’s a brainstorming session. We get together to chew on ideas and this is specifically something that you want to do in a group. I mean, I tell people ahead of time we’re chewing on ideas with each other, where we’re gonna, we’re gonna go over these. I always tell people ahead of time: be positive, let’s examine all of them for their pros and cons, I. I like it because you can really take an idea and you can pull it apart. You can see the pieces of it, um, think about how it will work with other systems in the game and put it back together, sometimes in a slightly different way.

item.title: Learning Lessons From Failure
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, so I’ve been telling a lot of stories.

I’m gonna consider this kind of a summary video. So far, I wanted to talk about lessons I’ve learned, and specifically for this video, I want to talk about the lessons I’ve learned by failing, not by success, because I think I’ve learned more from failure than From Success. A very specific example of that is: Fallout was very successful in many ways, but I did not realize parts of its success that I just stumbled into or got lucky with, as opposed to things that I was actually good at. So I left Fallout thinking, for example, I wrote the intro cinematic, the narration by Ron Perlman, the war, never changes intro. It turned out people really liked that and turned out to be really popular. It turned out to be enduring, so you know, a couple years later, maybe more like three years later, when I started making tempo of Elemental Evil, I thought: you know what I’m — a good writer. I’m gonna write dialogue, new. I am not a good writer, or to be specific, there are some things I write well and some things I write poorly.

item.title: How To Write Design Docs
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. I’ve told a lot of stories about game development, but today I actually want to talk about process, specifically how I make my design documents.

I want to talk both about the order I do them and what actually each one contains. So most of this applies to the original Ip that I’ve made, but it can work for other games. I mean, I’ve made d? D and vampire, but this is the process I go through when I’m creating an IP from scratch and I want to [Music], write documents, because I know once we get a team attached, people are going to want to know answers to a lot of things. So when I do my documentation for a new game, I always do them in a very particular order. I always start with setting, and then I do story and then I do system. So they all begin with S’s, but the reason I do it that way is setting kind of defines the space in.
